title: Howl's Moving Castle - Chapter Three: In which Sophie enters into a castle and a bargain (Fragment 3)
text:
Still, things were bound to be odd where wizards were concerned, Sophie thought. And this kitchen, or workshop, was beautifully cozy and very peaceful. Sophie went properly to sleep and snored. She did not wake up when there came a flash and a muted bang from the workbench, followed by a hurriedly bitten-off swear word from Michael. She did not wake when Michael, sucking his burned fingers, put the spell aside for the night and fetched bread and cheese out of the closet. She did not stir when Michael knocked her stick down with a clatter, reaching over her for a log to put on the fire, or when Michael, looking down into Sophie’s open mouth, remarked to the fireplace, “She’s got all her teeth. She’s not the Witch of the Waste, is she?”

“I wouldn’t have let her come in if she was,” the fireplace retorted.

Michael shrugged and picked Sophie’s stick politely up again. Then he put a log on the fire with equal politeness and went away to bed somewhere overhead.

In the middle of the night Sophie was woken by someone snoring. She jumped upright, rather irritated to discover that she was the one who had been snoring. It seemed to her that she had only dropped off for a second or so, but Michael seemed to have vanished in those seconds, taking the light with him. No doubt a wizard’s apprentice learned to do that kind of thing in his first week. And he had left the fire very low. It was giving out irritating hissings and poppings. A cold draft blew on Sophie’s back. Sophie recalled that she was in a wizard’s castle, and also, with unpleasant distinctness, that there was a human skull on a workbench somewhere behind her.

She shivered and cranked her stiff old neck around, but there was only darkness behind her. “Let’s have a bit more light, shall we?” she said. Her cracked little voice seemed to make no more noise than the crackling of the fire. Sophie was surprised. She had expected it to echo through the vaults of the castle. Still, there was a basket of logs beside her. She stretched out a creaking arm and heaved a log on the fire, which sent a spray of green and blue sparks flying up the chimney. She heaved on a second log and sat back, not without a nervous look or so behind her, where blue-purple light from the fire was dancing over the polished brown bone of the skull. The room was quite small. There was no one in it but Sophie and the skull.

“He’s got both feet in the grave and I’ve only got one,” she consoled herself. She turned back to the fire, which was now flaring up into blue and green flames. “Must be salt in that wood,” Sophie murmured. She settled herself more comfortably, putting her knobby feet on the fender and her head into a corner of the chair, where she could stare into the colored flames, and began dreamily considering what she ought to do in the morning. But she was sidetracked a little by imagining a face in the flames. “It would be a thin blue face,” she murmured, “very long and thin, with a thin blue nose. But those curly green flames on top are most definitely your hair. Suppose I didn’t go until Howl gets back? Wizards can lift spells, I suppose. And those purple flames near the bottom make the mouth—you have savage teeth, my friend. You have two green tufts of flame for eyebrows....” Curiously enough, the only orange flames in the fire were under the green eyebrow flames, just like eyes, and they each had a little purple glint in the middle that Sophie could almost imagine was looking at her, like the pupil of an eye. “On the other hand,” Sophie continued, looking into the orange flames, “if the spell was off, I’d have my heart eaten before I could turn around.”

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Three: In which Sophie enters into a castle and a bargain

vocabulary.definition: Quickly or abruptly cut or ended, especially speech or words.
vocabulary.example: A hurriedly bitten-off swear word.

vocabulary.definition: Uncomfortably cool or cold.
vocabulary.example: A cold draft blew on Sophie’s back.

vocabulary.definition: To comfort someone who is sad or disappointed.
vocabulary.example: She consoled herself by saying, “He’s got both feet in the grave.”

vocabulary.definition: Giving a feeling of comfort, warmth, and relaxation.
vocabulary.example: This kitchen was beautifully cozy and very peaceful.

vocabulary.definition: Having surface lines or marks caused by breaking or damage.
vocabulary.example: Her cracked little voice seemed to make no more noise than the crackling of the fire.

vocabulary.definition: Making a sharp, high sound when something moves or is moved.
vocabulary.example: She stretched out a creaking arm and heaved a log on the fire.

vocabulary.definition: A current of unpleasantly cold air blowing through a room.
vocabulary.example: A cold draft blew on Sophie’s back.

vocabulary.definition: To fall asleep.
vocabulary.example: It seemed as if she had only dropped off for a second or so.

vocabulary.definition: A low metal frame in front of a fireplace to prevent ash or coal from falling out.
vocabulary.example: She put her feet on the fender.

vocabulary.definition: The visible, gaseous part of a fire.
vocabulary.example: Blue and green flames danced in the fire.

vocabulary.definition: Burning brightly with a sudden intensity.
vocabulary.example: The fire was now flaring up into blue and green flames.

vocabulary.definition: To lift or haul something heavy with great effort.
vocabulary.example: She heaved on a second log and sat back.

vocabulary.definition: With great speed, especially because of a lack of time.
vocabulary.example: A hurriedly bitten-off swear word from Michael.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling annoyed or bothered.
vocabulary.example: She jumped upright, rather irritated to discover she had been snoring.

vocabulary.definition: Having lumps or rounded protuberances.
vocabulary.example: Putting her knobby feet on the fender.

vocabulary.definition: To remove or cancel magical enchantments.
vocabulary.example: Wizards can lift spells, I suppose.

vocabulary.definition: Softened or toned down in sound or colour.
vocabulary.example: There came a flash and a muted bang from the workbench.

vocabulary.definition: Made smooth and shiny by rubbing.
vocabulary.example: Blue-purple light was dancing over the polished brown bone of the skull.

vocabulary.definition: Made a short, sharp sound like a small explosion.
vocabulary.example: The fire was giving out hissings and poppings.

vocabulary.definition: To reply to a remark in a sharp, angry, or witty manner.
vocabulary.example: The fireplace retorted, “I wouldn’t have let her come in if she was.”

vocabulary.definition: To shake slightly because you are cold, frightened, or ill.
vocabulary.example: She shivered and cranked her stiff old neck around.

vocabulary.definition: To raise your shoulders to show that you do not know or are not interested.
vocabulary.example: Michael shrugged and picked Sophie’s stick up again.

vocabulary.definition: Distracted or diverted from the main subject or activity.
vocabulary.example: She was sidetracked a little by imagining a face in the flames.

vocabulary.definition: The bony structure that forms the head and protects the brain.
vocabulary.example: There was a human skull on the workbench behind her.

vocabulary.definition: To move slightly or make a small movement.
vocabulary.example: She did not stir when Michael knocked her stick down.

vocabulary.definition: Disappeared suddenly and completely.
vocabulary.example: Michael seemed to have vanished in those seconds.

vocabulary.definition: A person who has magical powers.
vocabulary.example: Wizards can lift spells, I suppose.
